APT29 Threat Intelligence Report — Q2 2025

Executive Summary

In this campaign, APT29 (also known as Cozy Bear), a threat actor attributed to the Russian Foreign Intelligence Service (SVR), targeted government agencies and defense contractors across Europe and North America.

The group leveraged spearphishing emails (T1566.001) containing malicious attachments to gain initial access. Once inside the network, they deployed a custom version of Cobalt Strike as a Command and Control (C2) framework.

Indicators of Compromise (IoCs)

The following network indicators were observed during this campaign:

Command and Control servers:
- 185.220.101.45
- 91.108.4.234
- evil-c2-domain.ru
- update-service.net

Malicious files:
- MD5:  d41d8cd98f00b204e9800998ecf8427e
- SHA256: e3b0c44298fc1c149afbf4c8996fb92427ae41e4649b934ca495991b7852b855
- SHA1: da39a3ee5e6b4b0d3255bfef95601890afd80709

Email indicators:
- phishing@malicious-domain.com

Malware Analysis

The payload identified as WellMess malware (also known as WellMail) was used to establish persistence on compromised systems. This malware uses encrypted communications over HTTP/HTTPS and leverages the following techniques:

- T1059.001 — PowerShell execution for lateral movement
- T1071.001 — Web Protocols for C2 communication
- T1547.001 — Registry Run Keys for persistence

Vulnerability Exploitation

APT29 exploited CVE-2021-40444 (Microsoft MSHTML Remote Code Execution) to deliver the initial payload. Additionally, CVE-2020-1472 (Zerologon) was used for privilege escalation within the compromised domain.

Attribution and Context

This activity cluster is attributed to APT29 with high confidence. The group primarily targets the energy sector and government institutions in Germany, France, and the United States.

Recommendations

Security teams should apply patches for CVE-2021-40444 and CVE-2020-1472 immediately and monitor for connections to the listed C2 infrastructure. Network detection rules should flag communications with 185.220.101.45 and evil-c2-domain.ru.
